documents.title: Infection Triage Priorities
documents.content: Escalate rapidly for signs of severe infection: persistent high fever, confusion, breathing difficulty, rapidly spreading redness, severe dehydration, or inability to keep fluids down. Early recognition and supportive care can prevent deterioration when formal care is delayed.

documents.title: Wound Cleaning and Monitoring
documents.content: Clean wounds with safe water or sterile saline, remove visible debris, and apply clean dressings. Re-check daily for increased pain, swelling, heat, pus, red streaking, or fever. Do not close contaminated wounds unless directed by a qualified clinician.

documents.title: Oral Rehydration for GI Illness
documents.content: For diarrhea or vomiting, focus on hydration early. Standard oral rehydration mix: 1 liter safe water, 6 level teaspoons sugar, 0.5 level teaspoon salt. Give small frequent sips and monitor urine output, mental status, and ability to drink.

documents.title: Dental Emergency Stabilization
documents.content: Dental infections can become systemic emergencies. Prioritize pain control, gentle oral hygiene, and urgent referral pathways. Track swelling progression, fever, and swallowing/breathing changes as high-risk warning signs.

documents.title: Pregnancy and Postpartum Red Flags
documents.content: Seek urgent help for heavy bleeding, severe abdominal pain, persistent high blood pressure symptoms, fever, seizures, or reduced fetal movement. Prepare clean delivery and newborn warming supplies in advance, but escalate quickly when complications appear.

documents.title: Medication and Antimicrobial Stewardship
documents.content: Do not use antibiotics casually or partially. Use them only when clearly indicated and from reliable guidance to reduce harm and resistance risk. Track drug name, dose, timing, allergies, and response in a written log.
